1.label: Question 1
1.scenario: ABC Enterprises is a manufacturing concern which presently employs around 1200 people at various levels. It is having a Group Health Policy with the XYZ Insurance Ltd. for all its employees and their family members. The Group Health Policy is due for renewal in one month from now. The underlying benefits in Group Health policy include reimbursement of inpatient and outpatient bills and are tiered (becomes more liberal as the grade of employee increases). There is no waiting period on claims for maternity or pre-existing diseases. The quote of XYZ insurer for next year is for Rs. 1.68 Crore (including service tax). The CFO of the ABC Enterprises feels that it is exorbitant.

i.question: Describe the reasons for difference in claims under existing policy and the quote of premium submitted by XYZ Insurance Ltd. for the next year.

Indicative Solution: The claims are for 11 months as one month is still to go for the policy to be complete.
There will be some claims which are incurred but not reported as on that. Hence, insurer needs to account for the IBNR.

The no. of employees in the existing policy is 1125 while it is given that ABC Enterprises presently employs around 1200 people. Moreover, not only the no. of employees but the total demographic of all the lives covered including family members needs to be compared.
There will be loading for medical inflation for next year to be provided in computation of premium.
All the people covered under existing policy will be older by one year for policy renewal and this impact has to be considered while arriving at premium for next year.

XYZ insurer may not rely 100% on the performance of this policy to rate it. They may be having experience of other policies with similar benefits and may like to use the data available to them for entire portfolio which will be more credible because of bigger pool of lives insured.
Broker is involved and hence the insurer needs to charge for brokerage they paid to concerned broker.
Insurer needs to charge for various expenses, like claims handling, policy administration, other management expenses, etc.
There can be loading for re-insurance cost, cost of capital, catastrophic reserve, etc.
Insurer needs to provide for its profit and contingency margin.
There is service tax in the premium that insurer needs to collect from customer and deposit.

ii.question: Discuss the merits of proposal put forth by CFO to retain the risk internally.

Indicative Solution: Advantages of self-retention: Company may tailor made the benefits as per the requirements. Company will be benefitted by favorable loss experience. It will have better control on claims adjudication. It can avoid payment of brokerage, expenses & profit of insurance company, tax on premium, etc.

Disadvantages: There will be fluctuations in the claims experience from year to year and the budget provisions of last year's claims plus 10% may not be adequate to meet the claims outgo in some of the years. Company may not have in house expertise to design the policy benefits. There may be spurt of claims because of sudden outburst of disease, etc. and company may not be protected against that. The company may not have required expertise to handle claims. There is also danger of collusion by employees for payment claims fraudulently. There can be tax implications for payment made by the employer to employees. The administration of the policy in house may be more costly because of lack of economies of scale. The company may not be able to deal with so many hospitals as insurance company can.

iii.question: List down the changes in policy design that will bring down the overall premium of the Group Health Policy in question.

Indicative Solution: Coverage to only employees not their family members
Lowering the sum insured
Changing the individual sum insured to family floater
Introducing co-pay on claims
Introducing a deductible on every claim
Removing outpatient cover completely or lowering the limit
Put disease specific restrictions on claims
Removing maternity from the benefit altogether or introducing waiting period
Removing coverage of pre-existing diseases completely or introducing waiting period
Instead of tiered benefits that increases with the grade have only base cover for employees at all level
Restriction on room category insured can avail

2.label: Question 2
2.scenario: Following data is extracted from the books of the two medium sized general insurance companies that are dealing in only health insurance. Insurer A: Available Assets (Approved) Policyholder's Funds 24300, Liabilities 19400, Shareholder's Funds 17200, Liabilities 12100. Gross Written Premium 36400, Net Written Premium 32800, Gross Incurred Claims 22200, Net Incurred Claims 20800. Insurer B: Available Assets (Approved) Policyholder's Funds 24500, Liabilities 19400, Shareholder's Funds 15700, Liabilities 11200. Gross Written Premium 34700, Net Written Premium 22000, Gross Incurred Claims 28000, Net Incurred Claims 19700. The minimum Required Solvency Margin is Rs. 50 Crore. The benefit of reinsurance for Solvency Margin Calculation is restricted to 25% only in case of health insurance.

i.question: Please compute Solvency Ratio of Insurers A and B as per the IRDA (Assets, Liabilities and Solvency Margin of Insurer Regulations.

Indicative Solution: Solvency Ratio = Available Solvency Margin / Required Solvency Margin

For Insurer A: Available Solvency Margin = (24300 - 19400) + (17200 - 12100) = 4900 + 5100 = 10000. Required Solvency Margin = max(50 crore, 20% of 32800) = max(50, 6560) = 6560. Solvency Ratio = 10000/6560 = 152.44%

For Insurer B: Available Solvency Margin = (24500 - 19400) + (15700 - 11200) = 5100 + 4500 = 9600. Required Solvency Margin = max(50 crore, 20% of 22000) = max(50, 4400) = 4400. Solvency Ratio = 9600/4400 = 218.18%

ii.question: Suggest the measures to improve the Solvency Ratios applicable to Insurers A and B.

Indicative Solution: Increase capital through fresh infusion or retained earnings
Reduce risk exposure through reinsurance
Improve underwriting to reduce claim ratios
Invest in assets with better liquidity
Proper reinsurance arrangements to optimize RSM benefit
Reduce expenses to improve profitability
Proper investment strategy to maximize returns

3.label: Question 3
3.scenario: Following is the extract from the PMI product portfolio of a General Insurance Company. Find out the missing values from A to F in the table given below: Age 0-35: Group Incidence Rate 4.3%, Average Claim Size 20800, Burning Cost per Capita ?, Premium per Life ?, Loss Ratio ?. Age 36-60: Group Incidence Rate ?, Average Claim Size 42500, Burning Cost per Capita 3060, Premium per Life ?, Loss Ratio 55.9%. Age >60: Group Incidence Rate 13.7%, Average Claim Size ?, Burning Cost per Capita 7836, Premium per Life ?, Loss Ratio 73.8%.

i.question: Find out the missing values from A to F in the table.
Explain the meaning of the following terms related to Health Insurance: a) Budget policies b) Capitation c) Free cover d) ICD

Indicative Solution: Budget policies: Low cost health insurance policies with limited coverage designed for economically weaker sections.
Capitation: Payment model where insurer pays a fixed amount per enrolled member per year to the provider regardless of services rendered.
Free cover: Coverage provided without payment of premium, usually for dependent children or in special schemes.
ICD: International Classification of Diseases - standard coding system for diseases and conditions used in health insurance.

5.label: Question 5
5.scenario: You are pricing actuary of a General Insurance Company in India and requested to price for a RSBY (Rasthria Swasthya Bima Yojna) renewal tender. The scheme is due for the second renewal. Your company did not participate in the scheme before.

i.question: Describe the most important pricing factors you should consider in your risk premium pricing. Please especially relate your answer to this RSBY tender and give reasons why the factors described are of relevance.

Indicative Solution: Epidemic potential: BPL population may have different health profile. Limited enrollment suggests selection. Provider network and quality. Medical inflation. Claim investigation and fraud control. Administrative costs. Reinsurance arrangements.

ii.question: Which pricing factor seems of special importance related to this scheme? Give reasons for your explanation.

Indicative Solution: Anti-selection is of special importance because enrollment is voluntary and only 38000 out of 70000 BPL families enrolled. Those who enrolled may have higher propensity to claim.

iii.question: The data provided above are not comprehensive and not ideal to arrive at risk adequate rates. Which additional information would be important and relevant to arrive at risk rates?

Indicative Solution: Complete enrollment data. Claims development beyond October 2011. Age-wise distribution of claims. Provider-wise claims. Pre and post hospitalization costs. Reinsurance terms. Administrative cost breakdown. Industry experience of similar schemes.

iv.question: Your actuarial colleague is of the opinion that the data basis for a quotation is not adequate and the company should not participate on such a tender since it appears too risky. What are potential reasons for still participating on the tender?

Indicative Solution: Strategic entry into government business. Building relationship with government. Portfolio diversification. Learning opportunity. Long-term visibility.

i.question: What are the three major catastrophic risks a Health Insurer is facing?

Indicative Solution: Epidemic/Pandemic outbreak. Large concentration of high cost claims. Natural catastrophe affecting healthcare infrastructure.

ii.question: How can the health insurance company protect itself or mitigate such risks?

Indicative Solution: Reinsurance arrangements. Catastrophe bonds. Diversification of portfolio. Proper underwriting. Monitoring of large claims. Capital reserves. Scenario analysis.

iii.question: Based on data related to the Spanish Flu outbreak in 1918, it is known that per 1000 population about 25 were required to get hospitalized due to this disease within a 12 month period. Please roughly estimate the impact it would have on a portfolio in India running at a loss ratio of 80%.

Indicative Solution: Hospitalization rate of 25 per 1000 = 2.5%. If loss ratio is 80%, additional 2.5% claims would increase loss ratio to 82.5%. Or if we assume claims increase by 3% (2.5/80 x 100), the loss ratio would jump to 83%.

7.label: Question 7
7.scenario: You are the actuarial member of a product development and product enhancement team which plans to improve the inpatient flagship product for individuals. This product has no cashless facility and no sub-limits.

i.question: What are the risks if the entry age is increased from 65 to 85 years? What are the risks if the expiry age of 85 is waived and renewal is guaranteed for life? What are the risks to waive the waiting period and underwriting for newborn babies in case the parents are already insured for more than 4 years?

Indicative Solution: Risks of increasing entry age from 65 to 85: Higher mortality and morbidity. Less time to recover initial expenses. Higher lapse risk. Anti-selection.
Risks of waiving expiry age and guaranteeing renewal: Accumulation of old ages. Unknown future medical costs. Regulatory risks. Pricing uncertainty.
Risks of waiving waiting period and underwriting for newborn: Parents may add newborn only when sick. High claim frequency in first year. Adverse selection. No data for pricing.
